factors.summary: The airline industry is subject to extensive government regulation, which imposes significant costs and operational restrictions.

evidence.text: Our business is subject to extensive government regulation, which may result in increases in our costs, disruptions to our operations, limits on our operating flexibility, reductions in the demand for air travel, and competitive disadvantages.
evidence.subsection: We rely on third-party distribution channels and must manage effectively the costs, rights and functionality of these channels.

factors.summary: The airline industry is heavily taxed, impacting revenue and profitability.

evidence.text: The airline industry is subject to extensive government fees and taxation that negatively impact our revenue and profitability. The U.S. airline industry is one of the most heavily taxed of all industries.
evidence.subsection: The airline industry is heavily taxed.

factors.summary: The company faces challenges in retaining key personnel, which could adversely affect its business.

evidence.text: We believe that our future success will depend in large part on our ability to retain or attract highly qualified management, technical and other personnel. Any inability to retain or attract significant numbers of qualified management and other personnel would have a material adverse effect on our business, results of operations and financial condition.
evidence.subsection: The loss of key personnel upon whom we depend to operate our business or the inability to attract additional qualified personnel could adversely affect our business.

factors.summary: The competitive environment is intensifying with new entrants, aggressive rivals, and commoditization.

evidence.text: Our competitors include other major domestic airlines and foreign, regional and new entrant airlines, as well as joint ventures formed by some of these airlines, many of which have more financial or other resources and/or lower cost structures than ours, as well as other forms of transportation, including rail and private automobiles.
evidence.subsection: The airline industry is intensely competitive and dynamic.

factors.summary: The company is facing pressure on its market share due to competition from low-cost carriers and state-owned airlines.

evidence.text: Low-cost carriers (including so-called ultra-low cost carriers) have a profound impact on industry revenues. Using the advantage of low unit costs, these carriers offer lower fares in order to shift demand from larger, more established airlines, and represent significant competitors, particularly for customers who fly infrequently and are price sensitive and tend not to be loyal to any one particular carrier.
evidence.subsection: The airline industry is intensely competitive and dynamic.

factors.summary: The company faces significant pension and postretirement benefit funding obligations that may affect its financial health.

evidence.text: Our pension funding obligations are significant. The amount of these obligations will depend on the performance of investments held in trust by the pension plans, interest rates for determining liabilities and actuarial experience. The minimum funding obligation applicable to our pension plans was subject to favorable temporary funding rules that expired at the end of 2017 and, as a result, our minimum pension funding obligations increased materially beginning in 2019. In addition, we may have significant obligations for other postretirement benefits, retiree medical and other postretirement benefits.
evidence.subsection: We have significant pension and other postretirement benefit funding obligations, which may adversely affect our liquidity, results of operations and financial condition.

factors.summary: The company faces significant geopolitical risks, including conflicts, terrorist attacks, and political instability, which can impact travel demand and costs.

evidence.text: Acts of terrorism or fear of such attacks, including elevated national threat warnings, wars or other military conflicts, may depress air travel, particularly on international routes, and cause declines in revenues and increases in costs.
evidence.subsection: We may be adversely affected by conflicts overseas or terrorist attacks; the travel industry continues to face ongoing security concerns.

factors.summary: The company is subject to environmental regulations, including those related to GHG emissions, which could increase operating costs.

evidence.text: There is increasing global regulatory focus on climate change and greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. Efforts by the EU in 2009 to regulate flights arriving from or departing for airports outside of the EU have been postponed as members of the International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) implement a global agreement on GHG emissions from the aviation sector.
evidence.subsection: We are subject to risks associated with climate change, including increased regulation to reduce emissions of greenhouse gases.

factors.summary: International freight volumes are declining, impacting cargo revenue.

evidence.text: Cargo revenue decreased $101 million, or 13.5%,...primarily due to a decrease in cargo ton miles reflecting declines in international freight volumes.
evidence.subsection: Nine Months Ended September 30, 2019 Compared to Nine Months Ended September 30, 2018

factors.summary: Productivity improvements are limited, with maintenance costs increasing due to contract changes.

evidence.text: Maintenance, materials and repairs increased 16.4%...primarily due to a contract change that resulted in certain flight equipment transitioning to a flight hour based contract...An increase in the volume of airframe and engine overhauls performed under time and material based contracts as well as an increase in the volume of component part repairs also drove higher maintenance expenses.
evidence.subsection: Nine Months Ended September 30, 2019 Compared to Nine Months Ended September 30, 2018

factors.summary: Route network expansion is focused on increasing capacity at key hubs.

evidence.text: Other rent and landing fees increased 7.2% primarily due to an expansion at DFW that became fully operational in May 2019 and rate increases at certain hub airports.
evidence.subsection: Nine Months Ended September 30, 2019 Compared to Nine Months Ended September 30, 2018

factors.summary: AAG's capital deployment program, including share repurchases and dividends, could be affected by changes in interest rates, impacting their financial flexibility.

evidence.text: Our capital deployment program could increase the volatility of the price of our common stock and diminish our cash reserves. ... The timing and amount of repurchases, if any, will be subject to market and economic conditions, applicable legal requirements and other relevant factors.
evidence.subsection: The price of AAG common stock has recently been and may in the future be volatile.

factors.summary: AAG's forward guidance is cautious, with no specific changes mentioned, but their capital deployment program is ongoing with remaining authority.

evidence.text: We cannot guarantee that we will continue to repurchase our common stock or pay dividends on our common stock or that our capital deployment program will enhance long-term stockholder value.
evidence.subsection: The price of AAG common stock has recently been and may in the future be volatile.

factors.summary: The revenue mix is shifting towards higher-margin passenger services, with strong performance in international regions like Latin America, though there is a notable decline in cargo revenue. This shift has contributed to an overall increase in total operating revenues despite the decrease in cargo services.

evidence.text: Latin America was the best performing international region with PRASM increasing 5.9%, while Atlantic PRASM declined 4.6%
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended September 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended September 30, 2018

evidence.text: Passenger revenue increased $949 million, or 3.1%,...Cargo revenue decreased $101 million, or 13.5%,...Total operating revenues increased $855 million, or 2.5%
evidence.subsection: Nine Months Ended September 30, 2019 Compared to Nine Months Ended September 30, 2018

factors.summary: Organic growth is accelerating, driven by passenger revenue increases and higher PRASM, with evidence of improving margins and operational changes contributing to stable revenue growth.

evidence.text: Passenger revenue increased $434 million, or 4.1%, driven by higher RPMs and load factor. Domestic PRASM increased 4.0%
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended September 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended September 30, 2018

evidence.text: Three Months Ended September 30, Increase (Decrease) 2019 2018 Passenger revenue $10,995 $10,561 $434 4.1 Cargo revenue 208 260 (52) (19.9) Other operating revenue 708 738 (30) (4.1) Total operating revenues 11,911 11,559 352 3.0
evidence.subsection: Financial Overview

evidence.text: Total operating revenues increased $855 million, or 2.5%,...passenger revenue as described above
evidence.subsection: Nine Months Ended September 30, 2019 Compared to Nine Months Ended September 30, 2018

factors.summary: Overall demand is consistently accelerating across all periods, supported by increases in RPMs and load factors. The evidence from various subsections uniformly indicates strong and increasing passenger demand.

evidence.text: Passenger demand resulted in a 4.9% quarter-over-quarter increase in RPMs and a 3.2 point increase in passenger load factor.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended September 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended September 30, 2018

evidence.text: The increase in passenger revenue in the third quarter of 2019 was due to continued strength in passenger demand resulting in a 4.9% quarter-over-quarter increase in revenue passenger miles (RPMs) and a 3.2 point increase in passenger load factor.
evidence.subsection: Revenue

evidence.text: Passenger revenue increased $949 million, or 3.1%, in the first nine months of 2019 from the first nine months of 2018 primarily due to continued strength in passenger demand resulting in a 3.8% period-over-period increase in RPMs and a 2.8 point increase in passenger load factor.
evidence.subsection: Nine Months Ended September 30, 2019 Compared to Nine Months Ended September 30, 2018

evidence.text: Passenger revenue increased $949 million, or 3.1%, primarily due to continued strength in passenger demand resulting in a period-over-period increase in RPMs and an increase in passenger load factor.
evidence.subsection: Nine Months Ended September 30, 2019 Compared to Nine Months Ended September 30, 2018

factors.summary: Input costs, particularly fuel costs, are decreasing, which is beneficial for margins. Fuel costs are decreasing due to lower average fuel prices. Fuel costs decreased due to lower prices, partially offsetting cost increases elsewhere.

evidence.text: Aircraft fuel and related taxes decreased $245 million, or 11.0%, due to lower average fuel prices.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended September 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended September 30, 2018

evidence.text: Our mainline and regional fuel expense totaled $2.5 billion in the third quarter of 2019, which was $266 million, or 9.7%, lower compared to the third quarter of 2018. This decrease was primarily driven by an 11.1% decrease in the average price per gallon of fuel including related taxes to $2.05 in the third quarter of 2019 from $2.30 in the third quarter of 2018.
evidence.subsection: Fuel

evidence.text: The period-over-period increase in our pre-tax income was principally driven by higher revenues and lower fuel costs, offset in part by increases in salaries, wages and benefits, maintenance expenses, and costs associated with increased regional capacity.
evidence.subsection: Nine Months Ended September 30, 2019 Compared to Nine Months Ended September 30, 2018

evidence.text: Mainline aircraft fuel and related taxes decreased 6.4%...due to a 6.6% decrease in the average price per gallon of fuel...Regional aircraft fuel and related taxes increased 1.8%...offset in part by a 5.7% decrease in the average price per gallon.
evidence.subsection: Nine Months Ended September 30, 2019 Compared to Nine Months Ended September 30, 2018

factors.summary: Labor costs are consistently increasing, creating margin pressure across different periods and contributing to higher operational expenses.

evidence.text: Salaries, wages, and benefits increased $88 million, or 2.8%, driven by higher expenses.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended September 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended September 30, 2018

evidence.text: The period-over-period increase in our pre-tax income was principally driven by higher revenues and lower fuel costs, offset in part by increases in salaries, wages and benefits, maintenance expenses, and costs associated with increased regional capacity.
evidence.subsection: Nine Months Ended September 30, 2019 Compared to Nine Months Ended September 30, 2018

evidence.text: Salaries, wages and benefits increased $269 million, or 2.9%,...Maintenance, materials and repairs increased $246 million, or 16.4%,...Other rent and landing fees increased $105 million, or 7.2%,
evidence.subsection: Nine Months Ended September 30, 2019 Compared to Nine Months Ended September 30, 2018

factors.summary: The company faces significant risks from economic conditions, including downturns, increased taxes, and volatility in fuel prices. These factors negatively impact demand for air travel, reduce operational cash flow, and contribute to stock price volatility.

evidence.text: An economic downturn or general global instability caused by military actions, terrorism, disease outbreaks, natural disasters or other causes could reduce the demand for air travel, which would reduce the amount of cash generated by operations.
evidence.subsection: Capital Raising Activity and Other Possible Actions

evidence.text: Increases in such taxes, fees and charges could negatively impact our business, results of operations and financial condition. Due to the competitive revenue environment, many increases in these fees and taxes have been absorbed by the airline industry rather than being passed on to the customer.
evidence.subsection: The airline industry is heavily taxed.

evidence.text: macro-economic conditions, including the price of fuel; ... These factors include general and industry-specific economic conditions.
evidence.subsection: The price of AAG common stock has recently been and may in the future be volatile.

factors.summary: The company's fuel costs are significantly influenced by price fluctuations, and while they have experienced a decrease in fuel prices, they are not hedging against future price increases, leaving them exposed to potential volatility.

evidence.text: As of September 30, 2019, we did not have any fuel hedging contracts outstanding.
evidence.subsection: Downturns in economic conditions could adversely affect our business.

evidence.text: Our current policy is not to enter into transactions to hedge our fuel consumption, although we review that policy from time to time based on market conditions and other factors.
evidence.subsection: Fuel

factors.summary: The company's operating margins are expanding, primarily due to lower fuel costs and higher revenues, though there are pressures from increased expenses in areas like maintenance and salaries. Additionally, the company's capital deployment program, including share repurchases and dividend payments, may impact future profitability by reducing cash reserves.

evidence.text: Total operating expenses increased $229 million, but CASM excluding fuel and special items increased 4.8%, driven by higher maintenance and selling expenses.

evidence.text: Total operating expenses increased $686 million, or 2.2%,...Total operating revenues increased $855 million, or 2.5%,
evidence.subsection: Nine Months Ended September 30, 2019 Compared to Nine Months Ended September 30, 2018

factors.summary: The company has significant debt obligations, which may strain its financial flexibility. However, it is actively managing its debt through refinancing and repurchasing activities, and the debt leverage appears to be stable with manageable refinancing.

evidence.text: The company is managing its debt, including refinancing and repurchasing debt to improve its balance sheet.
evidence.subsection: Capital Raising Activity and Other Possible Actions

evidence.text: We have significant amounts of indebtedness and other obligations, including pension obligations, obligations to make future payments on flight equipment and property leases related to airport and other facilities, and substantial non-cancelable obligations under aircraft and related spare engine purchase agreements.
evidence.subsection: We will need to obtain sufficient financing or other capital to operate successfully.

evidence.text: As of September 30, 2019, AAG had $24.8 billion in long-term debt...During the nine months ended September 30, 2019, there have been no material changes in our significant indebtedness...
evidence.subsection: Significant Indebtedness

evidence.text: changes in our level of outstanding indebtedness and other obligations; ... Our capital deployment program could increase the volatility of the price of our common stock and diminish our cash reserves.
evidence.subsection: The price of AAG common stock has recently been and may in the future be volatile.

factors.summary: The company's pricing power shows mixed results. While TRASM has been growing consistently, passenger yield increased but not enough to offset higher demand, suggesting limited success in raising prices.

evidence.text: Despite the negative impact to revenue due to Hurricane Dorian, our total revenue per available seat mile (TRASM) was 15.71 cents in the third quarter of 2019, a 2.0% increase as compared to 15.40 cents in the third quarter of 2018. This marks our twelfth consecutive quarter of positive TRASM growth.
evidence.subsection: Revenue

evidence.text: Passenger revenue increased $949 million, or 3.1%, in the first nine months of 2019 from the first nine months of 2018 primarily due to continued strength in passenger demand resulting in a 3.8% period-over-period increase in RPMs and a 2.8 point increase in passenger load factor. Passenger Yield was 16.04 cents in the first nine months of 2019, a 2.0% increase as compared to 15.72 cents in the first nine months of 2018.
evidence.subsection: Nine Months Ended September 30, 2019 Compared to Nine Months Ended September 30, 2018

factors.summary: The company faces significant litigation exposure, primarily due to ongoing union disputes and potential material litigation or investigations that could negatively impact its financial position and liquidity.

evidence.text: In addition, in May, we initiated litigation against the Transport Workers Union of America, AFL-CIO, International Association of Machinists and Aerospace Workers, and Airline Mechanic and Related Employee Association TWU/IAM, claiming that the unions have been engaged in an illegal work slowdown in an effort to influence contract negotiations.
evidence.subsection: Fleet and Operation

evidence.text: From time to time, we are a party to or otherwise involved in legal proceedings, claims and government inspections or investigations and other legal matters, both inside and outside the United States, arising in the ordinary course of our business or otherwise.
evidence.subsection: We may be a party to litigation in the normal course of business or otherwise, which could affect our financial position and liquidity.

factors.summary: The company is experiencing positive volume trends, as evidenced by consistent increases in revenue passenger miles (RPMs) and improvements in load factors across different periods. These trends indicate strong passenger demand and operational efficiency.

evidence.text: The increase in passenger revenue in the third quarter of 2019 was due to continued strength in passenger demand resulting in a 4.9% quarter-over-quarter increase in revenue passenger miles (RPMs) and a 3.2 point increase in passenger load factor.

factors.summary: The company is implementing a fleet strategy that involves modernizing its fleet with new aircraft orders, but this transition is accompanied by increased maintenance costs due to a shift to a flight hour-based contract.

evidence.text: Expenditures of $3.1 billion for property and equipment...including 15 Embraer E175 aircraft, eight Bombardier CRJ900 aircraft, six Airbus A321neo aircraft, four Boeing 737 MAX aircraft and two Boeing 787 Family aircraft.

evidence.text: Maintenance, materials and repairs per ASM increased 15.8% in the first nine months of 2019 as compared to the first nine months of 2018, primarily due to a contract change that resulted in certain flight equipment transitioning to a flight hour based contract (referred to as power by the hour) whereby expense is incurred and recognized based on actual hours flown.
evidence.subsection: AAG

factors.summary: The company has a significant order backlog of 264 aircraft scheduled for delivery through 2024, which indicates strong demand. However, there is no direct evidence linking this backlog to financial volatility or share repurchase activities.

evidence.text: As of September 30, 2019, we had definitive purchase agreements...Total 20 71 38 20 14 101 264

evidence.text: Since July 2014, our Board of Directors has approved seven share repurchase programs aggregating $13.0 billion of authority. As of September 30, 2019, there was $850 million of remaining authority to repurchase shares under our share repurchase programs.

factors.summary: The company has a substantial capital expenditure plan, primarily focused on aircraft purchases to modernize its fleet and enhance customer experience. Planned expenditures for 2019-2023 amount to approximately $8.4 billion, with specific allocations for various aircraft models.

evidence.text: Our business plan contemplates continued significant investments related to modernizing our fleet, improving the experience of our customers and updating our facilities. Significant capital resources will be required to execute this plan. We estimate that, based on our commitments as of September 30, 2019, our planned aggregate expenditures for aircraft purchase commitments and certain engines on a consolidated basis for calendar years 2019-2023 would be approximately $8.4 billion.
evidence.subsection: We will need to obtain sufficient financing or other capital to operate successfully.

While they are managing share repurchase programs as part of their cost reduction and efficiency strategies, the effectiveness of these programs may be limited by various factors.

factors.summary: Management's confidence is evident through their active share buyback program and consistent dividend payments. Despite potential risks and market conditions, they maintain their capital deployment strategy, indicating sustained belief in the company's value.

factors.summary: The company faces significant near-term risks, including potential holdbacks on cash due to credit card processing agreements, labor disruptions, interruptions at key facilities, delays in aircraft deliveries, and stock price volatility. These risks could affect liquidity, operations, financial performance, and investor confidence.

evidence.text: If our financial condition worsens, provisions in our credit card processing and other commercial agreements may adversely affect our liquidity. These holdback requirements can be modified at the discretion of the credit card processing companies upon the occurrence of specific events, including material adverse changes in our financial condition. An increase in the current holdbacks, up to and including 100% of relevant advanced ticket sales, would materially reduce our liquidity.
evidence.subsection: If our financial condition worsens, provisions in our credit card processing and other commercial agreements may adversely affect our liquidity.

evidence.text: Union disputes, employee strikes and other labor-related disruptions, or our inability to otherwise maintain labor costs at competitive levels may adversely affect our operations and financial performance.
evidence.subsection: If our financial condition worsens, provisions in our credit card processing and other commercial agreements may adversely affect our liquidity.

evidence.text: A significant interruption or disruption in service at one of our hubs, gateways or other airports where we have a significant presence, resulting from air traffic control (ATC) delays, weather conditions, natural disasters, growth constraints, performance by third-party service providers (such as electric utility or telecommunications providers), failure of computer systems, disruptions at airport facilities or other key facilities used by us to manage our operations (such as occurred in the United Kingdom at LGW on December 20, 2018 and LHR on January 8, 2019 due to unauthorized drone activity), labor relations, power supplies, fuel supplies, terrorist activities, or otherwise could result in the cancellation or delay of a significant portion of our flights and, as a result, could have a severe impact on our business, results of operations and financial condition.
evidence.subsection: Interruptions or disruptions in service at one of our key facilities could have a material adverse impact on our operations.

evidence.text: The extent of the delay to the scheduled deliveries of the Boeing 737 MAX aircraft...is expected to be impacted by...Boeing's production rate...In October 2019, the Office of the U.S. Trade Representative announced a 10% tariff on new Airbus aircraft imported from Europe.

factors.summary: AAG's free cash flow is showing improvement, driven by higher profitability and effective management of working capital. However, the company is utilizing this cash flow for share repurchases and dividends, which may impact future financial flexibility.

evidence.text: Net cash provided by operating activities was $3.2 billion and $2.8 billion for the first nine months of 2019 and 2018, respectively. The $398 million period-over-period increase in operating cash flows was primarily due to higher profitability...
evidence.subsection: AAG

evidence.text: Our capital deployment program could increase the volatility of the price of our common stock and diminish our cash reserves. ... The timing and amount of repurchases, if any, will be subject to market and economic conditions, applicable legal requirements and other relevant factors.
evidence.subsection: The price of AAG common stock has recently been and may in the future be volatile.
